lessons.title: Board Setup and Notation
lessons.content:
# Board Setup and Notation

## The Board
The chessboard is an 8x8 grid of 64 squares, alternating light and dark colors.

**Rule #1**: "White on right". The bottom-right square must be a light square.

- **Files**: Vertical columns (a-h)
- **Ranks**: Horizontal rows (1-8)

## Setup
- **A & H Files**: Rooks (R)
- **B & G Files**: Knights (N) - "Knights on the rim are dim" (usually)
- **C & F Files**: Bishops (B)
- **D File**: Queen (Q) - "Queen goes on her own color" (White Queen on light square, Black Queen on dark square)
- **E File**: King (K)

## Algebraic Notation
Every square has a name (coordinate): File + Rank.

Example: The bottom-left square is **a1**. The top-right is **h8**.

### Describing Moves
1. **Piece Letter** (K, Q, R, B, N). *Note: Pawns have no letter.*
2. **Destination Square**
3. **Examples**:
   - **Nf3**: Knight moves to f3
   - **e4**: Pawn moves to e4
   - **Bxc4**: Bishop *captures* on c4 (`x` means capture)
   - **O-O**: Kingside castle
   - **O-O-O**: Queenside castle
   - **+**: Check (e.g., Bb5+)
   - **#**: Checkmate (e.g., Qh5#)

## Key Ideas
- Memorize "White on right"
- Queen gets her color
- Coordinate training is essential for reading books
drills: Point to random squares and name them (e.g., 'Where is c6?')
Set up the board from scratch in under 1 minute correctly

lessons.title: Piece Moves and Captures
lessons.content:
# Piece Moves and Captures

## The Rook (R)
- Moves **horizontally** or **vertically** any number of squares
- Cannot jump over pieces
- Powerful piece (Value: 5 points)

## The Bishop (B)
- Moves **diagonally** any number of squares
- Stays on one color complex (Light-squared bishop vs Dark-squared bishop)
- Value: 3 points

## The Queen (Q)
- Combined power of Rook + Bishop
- Moves horizontal, vertical, diagonal
- Most valuable piece (except King)
- Value: 9 points

## The Knight (N)
- Moves in an **"L" shape**: 2 squares in one direction, then 1 square perpendicular
- **Special Ability**: The ONLY piece that can **jump** over others
- Value: 3 points

## The Pawn (P)
- Moves **forward only**
- First move: Can move 1 or 2 squares
- Later moves: Only 1 square
- **Captures**: Diagonally forward one square (Moves straight, eats diagonal)
- Value: 1 point
- *Promotion*: Reaches the end? Becomes a Q, R, B, or N

## The King (K)
- Moves 1 square in any direction
- Cannot move into "check" (danger)
- Value: Infinite (Lost king = Lost game)
- Combat strength: ~4 points in the endgame

## Captures
- You land on an opponent's piece and replace it
- You cannot capture your own pieces

## Key Ideas
- Knights jump
- Pawns are unique: Move straight, capture diagonal
- Center control (e4, d4, e5, d5) is crucial for piece mobility

## Common Mistakes
- Moving a Knight to a square it can't reach (e.g., trying to go 3 squares)
- Forgetting Pawns capture diagonally

positions.description: Knight on e4 - it can jump to 8 different squares!

drills: Place a Knight on e4. Find all 8 squares it can jump to
Place a Bishop on c1. See how many squares it controls. Move a pawn to d2. How many now? (Bad Piece!)

lessons.title: Check, Checkmate, Stalemate
lessons.content:
# Check, Checkmate, Stalemate

## Check
The King is under attack ("in check").

**You MUST get out of check immediately by:**
1. Moving the King to safety
2. Blocking the attack with another piece
3. Capturing the attacking piece

## Checkmate
The King is in check AND has no legal moves to escape.

**Game Over. The side in checkmate loses.**

## Stalemate
The King is NOT in check, but the player has no legal moves.

**Game Over. It's a DRAW (tie).**

This is a common beginner mistake - accidentally stalemating instead of checkmating!

## Key Ideas
- Check ≠ Checkmate (Check is just a warning)
- Stalemate = Draw (not a win!)
- Always verify your opponent has a legal move before claiming victory

positions.description: Checkmate! Black King on h8 has no escape
Stalemate! Black King is NOT in check but has no legal moves - it's a draw!

drills: Practice delivering checkmate with Queen + King vs lone King
Set up stalemate positions to recognize the pattern

lessons.title: Special Moves: Castling, En Passant, Promotion
lessons.content:
# Special Moves

## Castling (O-O or O-O-O)
A special King + Rook move that happens once per game.

**Kingside Castle (O-O)**:
- King moves 2 squares toward the h-file Rook
- Rook jumps over to the other side of the King

**Queenside Castle (O-O-O)**:
- King moves 2 squares toward the a-file Rook
- Rook jumps over to the other side of the King

**Requirements**:
1. King and Rook haven't moved yet
2. No pieces between them
3. King is not in check
4. King doesn't pass through or land on a square under attack

## En Passant
A special pawn capture.

If an enemy pawn moves 2 squares forward from its starting position and lands beside your pawn, you can capture it "in passing" on your next move only.

## Promotion
When a pawn reaches the opposite end of the board (rank 8 for White, rank 1 for Black), it MUST be promoted to a Queen, Rook, Bishop, or Knight.

**Almost always choose Queen!**

## Key Ideas
- Castle early for King safety
- En passant is rare but important to know
- Always promote to Queen (99% of the time)

positions.description: Both sides can castle kingside or queenside

drills: Learn the en passant rule with examples

lessons.title: Basic Tactics Introduction
lessons.content:
# Basic Tactics Introduction

Tactics are short-term sequences of moves that result in a tangible gain (winning a piece or checkmate). 99% of games under 1500 Elo are decided by tactics.

## The Most Basic Tactic: "Hanging Pieces"
A piece is "hanging" if it is unprotected and being attacked.

**Rules**:
- Always look at what your opponent's last move attacked
- Before you move, check if your square is safe

## Counting Attackers vs Defenders
If you want to capture something, count first.

- **Attackers**: Pieces trying to eat the target
- **Defenders**: Pieces protecting the target
- **Rule**: You can only win "material" if Attackers > Defenders
- *Exception*: If your Attacker is lower value than the Defender (e.g., Pawn attacks Queen), you can trade even if equal count

## The Checkmate Patterns (Introduction)

### Back Rank Mate
The King is trapped behind his own pawns. A Rook or Queen delivers checkmate on the back rank.

### Kiss of Death
The Queen is right next to the King, supported by another piece.

## Key Ideas
- **Undefended Pieces Drop Off** (John Nunn)
- Checks, Captures, Threats (CCT). Always look for these first

## Common Mistakes
- Not checking if your pieces are safe before moving
- Hanging pieces in one move

positions.description: Back Rank Mate: White plays Ra8# - Black King is trapped!

drills: Set up random positions. Instantly point to every 'Hanging Piece' for both sides
Practice checking 'Is it safe?' before every move in a game against a computer
